simpleExplanation: A tenant generally cannot challenge the landlord's title while enjoying the tenancy.
examples: A tenant disputes ownership of the landlord after entering into a lease.
A licensee challenges the licensor's authority after accepting the licence.

faqs.question: Can a tenant deny the landlord's ownership?
faqs.answer: Generally no, regarding the title existing at the commencement of the tenancy.

title: Estoppel of Acceptor of Bill of Exchange, Bailee or Licensee
officialText: Certain persons, including acceptors of bills of exchange, bailees and licensees, are prevented from denying specific facts that they previously accepted or acknowledged.
simpleExplanation: People who have accepted legal relationships or obligations cannot later deny the facts underlying those relationships.
examples: A bailee cannot deny the bailor's title to the goods.
An acceptor of a bill cannot deny the authority of the drawer.

faqs.question: Why are bailees subject to estoppel?
faqs.answer: Because they accepted possession based on the bailor's rights.

title: Who May Testify
officialText: All persons shall be competent to testify unless the Court considers that they are prevented from understanding questions or giving rational answers because of tender years, extreme old age, disease or similar causes.
simpleExplanation: Almost everyone can be a witness if they are capable of understanding questions and giving sensible answers.
examples: A child who understands questions may testify.
An elderly witness may testify if mentally capable.

faqs.question: Can children be witnesses?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they are capable of understanding and answering questions rationally.

faqs.question: Who decides witness competency?
faqs.answer: The Court.

title: Judges and Magistrates
officialText: No Judge or Magistrate shall be compelled to answer questions regarding his own conduct in Court or anything which came to his knowledge in Court in his judicial capacity, except upon the special order of a superior Court.
simpleExplanation: Judges and Magistrates cannot normally be forced to testify about their judicial actions or knowledge obtained while performing judicial duties.
examples: A Judge cannot be compelled to explain why a particular ruling was made.
A Magistrate cannot be questioned about confidential judicial deliberations.

faqs.question: Can a Judge be called as a witness about a case he decided?
faqs.answer: Generally no, unless a superior Court specifically orders it.

title: Communications During Marriage
officialText: No person who is or has been married shall be compelled or permitted to disclose any communication made during marriage by the spouse, except in specified circumstances such as proceedings between the spouses or cases involving offences committed by one spouse against the other.
simpleExplanation: Private communications between spouses are generally protected from disclosure in court.
examples: A husband cannot reveal confidential statements made by his wife during marriage.
A wife cannot disclose private marital communications without legal justification.

faqs.question: Are conversations between spouses confidential?
faqs.answer: Yes, they are generally protected by law.

faqs.question: Are there exceptions?
faqs.answer: Yes, such as disputes between spouses or crimes committed against one another.

title: Evidence as to Affairs of State
officialText: No person shall be permitted to give evidence derived from unpublished official records relating to affairs of State except with the permission of the department concerned.
simpleExplanation: Sensitive government records cannot be disclosed in court without proper authorization.
examples: Confidential national security documents.
Unpublished intelligence reports.

faqs.question: Can secret government records be disclosed in court?
faqs.answer: Only with appropriate governmental permission.

title: Official Communications
officialText: A public officer shall not be compelled to disclose communications made to him in official confidence when the public interest would suffer from such disclosure.
simpleExplanation: Government officials may withhold confidential official communications if disclosure would harm public interest.
examples: Confidential reports submitted to a government department.
Sensitive administrative communications.

faqs.question: Can a public officer refuse to reveal confidential communications?
faqs.answer: Yes, if disclosure would harm public interest.

title: Information as to Commission of Offences
officialText: No Magistrate, police officer or revenue officer shall be compelled to disclose the source of information regarding the commission of an offence.
simpleExplanation: Authorities are not required to reveal the identity of informants who report crimes.
examples: A police informant reports illegal activities.
An anonymous source provides information about a crime.

faqs.question: Can police be forced to reveal an informant's identity?
faqs.answer: Generally no.

title: Professional Communications
officialText: No advocate shall disclose communications made to him by or on behalf of a client in the course of professional employment, except in circumstances permitted by law.
simpleExplanation: Conversations between lawyers and clients are generally confidential.
examples: A client discusses legal strategy with an advocate.
A lawyer receives confidential case information.

faqs.question: Can a lawyer reveal confidential client information?
faqs.answer: Generally no, except in legally recognized circumstances.

title: Section 126 to Apply to Interpreters and Clerks
officialText: The protections relating to professional communications apply equally to interpreters, clerks and assistants employed by advocates.
simpleExplanation: People assisting lawyers must also keep client information confidential.
examples: A law firm's clerk handles confidential documents.
An interpreter assists during a legal consultation.

faqs.question: Do legal assistants have confidentiality obligations?
faqs.answer: Yes, the same privilege extends to them.

title: Privilege Not Waived by Volunteering Evidence
officialText: If a party gives evidence voluntarily, it does not automatically amount to consent for disclosure of privileged professional communications.
simpleExplanation: A person does not lose legal privilege merely by becoming a witness.
examples: A client testifies in court but does not waive lawyer-client privilege.
A witness voluntarily gives evidence while preserving confidential communications.

faqs.question: Does testifying waive legal privilege?
faqs.answer: Not automatically.

title: Confidential Communications with Legal Advisers
officialText: No person shall be compelled to disclose confidential communications made between him and his legal adviser unless he offers himself as a witness and certain conditions are satisfied.
simpleExplanation: Private communications with legal advisers are protected from disclosure.
examples: A client seeks legal advice regarding a dispute.
Confidential legal consultation records.

faqs.question: Can a court force disclosure of legal advice?
faqs.answer: Generally no, because legal communications are privileged.

title: Production of Title Deeds of Witness Not a Party
officialText: No witness who is not a party to a proceeding shall be compelled to produce title deeds, documents relating to property or documents that may incriminate him, unless he has agreed in writing to do so.
simpleExplanation: A witness who is not involved in the case cannot generally be forced to produce personal title documents.
examples: A third party is asked to produce property ownership documents.
A witness refuses to provide documents unrelated to the dispute.

faqs.question: Can a non-party witness be forced to produce title deeds?
faqs.answer: Generally no, unless legal conditions are met.

title: Production of Documents or Electronic Records Which Another Person Could Refuse to Produce
officialText: No person shall be compelled to produce documents or electronic records in his possession which another person would be entitled to refuse to produce if they were in that person's possession.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot be forced to produce documents that are protected by someone else's legal privilege.
examples: A clerk cannot be forced to produce privileged client documents.
An employee cannot disclose privileged legal communications belonging to the employer.

faqs.question: Can privilege be bypassed by obtaining documents from someone else?
faqs.answer: No. Privilege continues even when documents are held by another person.

title: Witness Not Excused from Answering on Ground That Answer May Criminate
officialText: A witness shall not be excused from answering a relevant question on the ground that the answer may incriminate him, but such answer shall not subject him to arrest, prosecution or be proved against him in any criminal proceeding, except for giving false evidence.
simpleExplanation: A witness must answer relevant questions, but receives protection against self-incrimination based on those answers.
punishment: Protection from prosecution except for perjury.
examples: A witness admits participation in a transaction under questioning.
A witness answers questions that may expose wrongdoing but receives statutory protection.

faqs.question: Can a witness refuse to answer because it may incriminate them?
faqs.answer: No, but the answer generally cannot be used against them later.

title: Accomplice
officialText: An accomplice shall be a competent witness against an accused person and a conviction is not illegal merely because it proceeds upon the uncorroborated testimony of an accomplice.
simpleExplanation: A person involved in the crime can testify against the accused and such testimony is legally admissible.
examples: One participant in a conspiracy testifies against another.
An accomplice provides evidence about the commission of an offence.

faqs.question: Can an accomplice be a witness?
faqs.answer: Yes, an accomplice is a competent witness.

title: Number of Witnesses
officialText: No particular number of witnesses shall in any case be required for the proof of any fact.
simpleExplanation: A fact can be proved by a single credible witness; there is no minimum number of witnesses required.
examples: A conviction based on testimony of a single reliable eyewitness.
A civil claim proved through one credible witness.

faqs.question: Are multiple witnesses always required?
faqs.answer: No. Quality of evidence is more important than quantity.

title: Order of Production and Examination of Witnesses
officialText: The order in which witnesses are produced and examined shall be regulated by the law and practice relating to civil and criminal procedure.
simpleExplanation: Procedural laws determine the sequence in which witnesses are called and examined.
examples: Prosecution witnesses examined before defence witnesses.
Witness examination follows procedural court rules.

faqs.question: Who decides the order of witnesses?
faqs.answer: The applicable procedural law and the Court.

title: Judge to Decide as to Admissibility of Evidence
officialText: The Judge shall determine whether evidence is admissible and may require proof of relevant preliminary facts before admitting evidence.
simpleExplanation: The Judge decides whether evidence can be accepted and considered by the Court.
examples: A Judge decides whether a document is admissible.
A Court verifies authenticity before admitting evidence.

faqs.question: Who determines admissibility of evidence?
faqs.answer: The Judge.

title: Examination-in-Chief, Cross-Examination and Re-Examination
officialText: Examination of a witness by the party calling him is examination-in-chief; by the adverse party is cross-examination; and subsequent examination by the party calling him is re-examination.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the three stages of witness examination.
examples: A prosecution witness is cross-examined by the defence.
A witness is re-examined to clarify answers.

faqs.question: What is cross-examination?
faqs.answer: Questioning of a witness by the opposing party.

title: Order of Examinations
officialText: Witnesses shall first be examined-in-chief, then cross-examined, and then re-examined if necessary. Re-examination shall be directed to explanation of matters referred to in cross-examination.
simpleExplanation: The law prescribes the sequence of witness examination and limits re-examination to clarification.
examples: A witness clarifies an inconsistency raised during cross-examination.
The Court permits further questioning on new matters.

faqs.question: Can new matters be introduced during re-examination?
faqs.answer: Only with permission of the Court.

title: Cross-Examination of Person Called to Produce Document
officialText: A person summoned merely to produce a document does not become a witness by that fact alone and cannot be cross-examined unless called as a witness.
simpleExplanation: Someone who only brings a document to court is not automatically subject to cross-examination.
examples: A records custodian produces official records.
A bank officer submits account statements without giving testimony.

faqs.question: Can a person producing documents be cross-examined?
faqs.answer: Only if they are called and examined as a witness.

title: Witnesses to Character
officialText: Witnesses who testify regarding the character of a person may be cross-examined and re-examined like any other witness.
simpleExplanation: Character witnesses are subject to the same examination procedures as other witnesses.
examples: A witness testifies about the accused's reputation.
A character witness is cross-examined regarding their knowledge.

faqs.question: Can character witnesses be cross-examined?
faqs.answer: Yes. They are treated like any other witness.

title: Leading Questions
officialText: Any question suggesting the answer which the person putting it wishes or expects to receive is called a leading question.
simpleExplanation: A leading question hints at the answer the examiner wants the witness to give.
examples: You saw the accused running away, didn't you?
The agreement was signed on Monday, correct?

faqs.question: What is a leading question?
faqs.answer: A question that suggests its own answer.

title: When Leading Questions Must Not Be Asked
officialText: Leading questions shall not be asked in examination-in-chief or re-examination unless the Court permits them.
simpleExplanation: A lawyer generally cannot suggest answers to their own witness unless the Court allows it.
examples: A prosecutor cannot ordinarily ask leading questions to their own witness.
The Court may permit leading questions on introductory matters.

faqs.question: Can leading questions be asked in examination-in-chief?
faqs.answer: Only with the permission of the Court.

title: When Leading Questions May Be Asked
officialText: Leading questions may be asked in cross-examination.
simpleExplanation: A lawyer may ask questions suggesting answers while questioning the opposing witness.
examples: You never saw the accused at the scene, did you?
You signed the document voluntarily, correct?

faqs.question: Are leading questions allowed in cross-examination?
faqs.answer: Yes, they are generally permitted.

title: Evidence as to Matters in Writing
officialText: A witness may be questioned about matters reduced to writing, and if intended to contradict the witness by the writing, attention must first be drawn to those parts to be used for contradiction.
simpleExplanation: Before using a document to contradict a witness, the relevant portions must be shown to the witness.
examples: Using a prior statement to challenge a witness's testimony.
Referring to a written report during cross-examination.

faqs.question: Can a witness be contradicted using a written statement?
faqs.answer: Yes, after their attention is drawn to the relevant portion.

title: Cross-Examination as to Previous Statements in Writing
officialText: A witness may be cross-examined regarding previous written statements relevant to the matter in question.
simpleExplanation: Earlier statements made by a witness can be used during cross-examination.
examples: Comparing court testimony with a police statement.
Questioning inconsistencies in earlier affidavits.

faqs.question: Can prior statements be used in cross-examination?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially to test consistency and credibility.

title: Questions Lawful in Cross-Examination
officialText: A witness may be asked questions to test veracity, discover identity and position in life, or shake credibility by injuring character.
simpleExplanation: Cross-examination can explore a witness's truthfulness, background and credibility.
examples: Questions regarding a witness's bias.
Questions testing the witness's reliability.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of cross-examination?
faqs.answer: To test truthfulness, reliability and credibility.

title: When Witness to Be Compelled to Answer
officialText: If a question relates to a matter relevant to the proceeding, the witness is generally bound to answer it.
simpleExplanation: Witnesses must answer relevant questions unless protected by a legal privilege.
examples: A witness answering questions about facts directly related to the dispute.
A witness providing information about events they observed.

faqs.question: Can a witness refuse to answer a relevant question?
faqs.answer: Generally no, unless protected by law.

title: Court to Decide When Question Shall Be Asked and When Witness Compelled to Answer
officialText: The Court shall decide whether a witness should be compelled to answer questions affecting credibility but not directly relevant to the case.
simpleExplanation: Judges control whether certain sensitive credibility-related questions may be asked.
examples: Questions about past conduct unrelated to the case.
Questions affecting personal reputation.

faqs.question: Who decides whether a witness must answer certain questions?
faqs.answer: The Court.

title: Question Not to Be Asked Without Reasonable Grounds
officialText: No question intended to impeach a witness's character shall be asked unless there are reasonable grounds for believing the imputation to be true.
simpleExplanation: Lawyers cannot make baseless accusations against witnesses.
examples: An advocate cannot allege fraud without some basis.
Character attacks require reasonable grounds.

faqs.question: Can an advocate ask any question about a witness's character?
faqs.answer: No. There must be reasonable grounds for the allegation.

title: Procedure of Court in Case of Question Being Asked Without Reasonable Grounds
officialText: If the Court believes a question was asked without reasonable grounds, it may report the circumstances to the appropriate authority controlling the advocate or representative responsible.
simpleExplanation: Lawyers who ask improper questions without justification may face professional consequences.
punishment: Professional disciplinary action may be initiated.
examples: A lawyer makes a serious allegation against a witness without evidence.
The Court reports misconduct to the Bar Council.

faqs.question: What happens if a lawyer asks baseless questions?
faqs.answer: The Court may report the matter to the relevant disciplinary authority.

title: Indecent and Scandalous Questions
officialText: The Court may forbid any questions or inquiries which appear to be indecent or scandalous, unless they directly relate to facts in issue or are necessary to determine relevant facts.
simpleExplanation: Courts can stop offensive or scandalous questioning unless it is genuinely necessary for deciding the case.
examples: Questions about a witness's private life unrelated to the case.
Embarrassing allegations with no connection to the dispute.

faqs.question: Can a lawyer ask any personal question during cross-examination?
faqs.answer: No. The Court may prohibit indecent or scandalous questions.

title: Questions Intended to Insult or Annoy
officialText: The Court shall forbid any question which appears intended to insult or annoy, or which though proper in itself appears needlessly offensive in form.
simpleExplanation: Courts must stop questions that are asked merely to harass, insult or embarrass a witness.
examples: Using abusive language while questioning a witness.
Asking humiliating questions with no legal purpose.

faqs.question: Can the Court stop insulting questions?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court is required to prevent such questioning.

title: Exclusion of Evidence to Contradict Answers to Questions Testing Veracity
officialText: When a witness answers questions intended solely to test credibility, evidence generally cannot be introduced to contradict those answers, except in certain cases recognized by law.
simpleExplanation: A witness's answers to some credibility-related questions are usually final and cannot be challenged through separate evidence.
examples: Questions about unrelated past conduct.
Questions affecting only the witness's credibility.

faqs.question: Can contradictory evidence always be produced against a witness's answer?
faqs.answer: No. Certain answers regarding credibility cannot generally be contradicted.

title: Question by Party to His Own Witness
officialText: The Court may, in its discretion, permit the person who calls a witness to put questions that might be asked in cross-examination by the adverse party.
simpleExplanation: If a witness becomes hostile, the party who called the witness may cross-examine that witness with the Court's permission.
examples: A prosecution witness changes their version in Court.
A witness refuses to support the party who called them.

faqs.question: What is a hostile witness?
faqs.answer: A witness who becomes adverse to the party who called them.

title: Impeaching Credit of Witness
officialText: The credit of a witness may be impeached by evidence showing that the witness is unworthy of credit, has been bribed, or has made previous inconsistent statements.
simpleExplanation: A witness's credibility can be challenged using legally admissible evidence.
examples: Showing that a witness accepted a bribe.
Producing prior inconsistent statements.

faqs.question: How can a witness's credibility be challenged?
faqs.answer: By proving bias, corruption, prior inconsistent statements or other factors affecting reliability.

title: Questions Tending to Corroborate Evidence of Relevant Fact
officialText: When a witness testifies about a relevant fact, the witness may be questioned regarding surrounding circumstances that support or corroborate the testimony.
simpleExplanation: Questions may be asked to strengthen and confirm a witness's testimony.
examples: Asking where and when the witness saw the event.
Questioning a witness about related surrounding facts.

faqs.question: Why are corroborative questions asked?
faqs.answer: To strengthen confidence in the witness's testimony.

title: Former Statements of Witness May Be Proved to Corroborate Later Testimony
officialText: A witness's previous statements relating to the same fact, made at or about the time when the fact occurred or before a legally competent authority, may be proved to corroborate testimony.
simpleExplanation: Earlier statements can be used to support a witness's testimony in Court.
examples: A complaint made immediately after an incident.
A statement given during investigation.

faqs.question: Can previous statements support a witness's testimony?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they meet the legal requirements for corroboration.

title: What Matters May Be Proved in Connection with Proved Statement Relevant Under Sections 26 or 27
officialText: Whenever any statement relevant under specified provisions is proved, matters may also be proved in order to contradict or corroborate it or to test the accuracy and truthfulness of the statement.
simpleExplanation: When certain statements are admitted into evidence, related facts may also be introduced to verify or challenge them.
examples: Testing the reliability of a dying declaration.
Corroborating an admission made by a party.

faqs.question: Can related facts be introduced to test a statement's reliability?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law permits corroboration and contradiction in such cases.

title: Refreshing Memory
officialText: A witness may refresh memory by referring to writings made by the witness at the time of the transaction or soon thereafter, or by referring to documents known to be accurate.
simpleExplanation: Witnesses may consult records to help remember details while testifying.
examples: A doctor refers to medical records.
A police officer consults investigation notes.

faqs.question: Can a witness look at notes while giving evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes, under circumstances permitted by law.

title: Testimony to Facts Stated in Document Mentioned in Section 159
officialText: A witness may testify to facts mentioned in documents used for refreshing memory even if the witness has no independent recollection of the facts, provided the witness is confident that the record was correctly made.
simpleExplanation: A witness may rely on accurate records even when personal memory has faded.
examples: A clerk testifies using regularly maintained records.
A medical professional relies on treatment notes.

faqs.question: Can a witness testify without independently remembering the event?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the witness trusts the accuracy of records made at the relevant time.

title: Right of Adverse Party as to Writing Used to Refresh Memory
officialText: Any writing referred to by a witness for the purpose of refreshing memory shall be produced and shown to the adverse party if required, and such party may cross-examine the witness thereon.
simpleExplanation: If a witness uses notes or records to refresh memory, the opposing party has the right to inspect those records and ask questions about them.
examples: A police officer refers to investigation notes while testifying.
A doctor refreshes memory using medical records.

faqs.question: Can the opposing party inspect documents used to refresh memory?
faqs.answer: Yes. The adverse party has a right to inspect and cross-examine regarding those documents.

title: Production of Documents
officialText: A witness summoned to produce a document shall, if it is in his possession or power, bring it to Court notwithstanding any objection as to its production or admissibility, and the Court shall decide such objection.
simpleExplanation: A witness must bring the required document to Court, and the Court will decide whether it can be used as evidence.
examples: A bank officer produces account records.
A company official brings requested business documents.

faqs.question: Can a witness refuse to bring a summoned document?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The witness must produce it, and the Court decides admissibility.

title: Giving, as Evidence, of Document Called for and Produced on Notice
officialText: When a party calls for a document and the document is produced and inspected, that party is bound to give it as evidence if the producing party so requires.
simpleExplanation: A party who requests and inspects a document may have to rely on it as evidence if the other party demands it.
examples: A plaintiff requests and inspects a contract produced by the defendant.
A party examines business records produced after notice.

faqs.question: What happens if a party inspects a document after calling for it?
faqs.answer: The party may be required to formally use it as evidence.

title: Using as Evidence of Document Production of Which Was Refused on Notice
officialText: When a party refuses to produce a document after notice, that party cannot afterwards use the document as evidence without consent of the other party or permission of the Court.
simpleExplanation: A party who refuses to produce a document cannot later rely on it unless allowed by the Court.
examples: A defendant withholds a document but later attempts to rely on it.
A party ignores a notice to produce records.

faqs.question: Can a withheld document later be used in Court?
faqs.answer: Only with consent of the other party or permission of the Court.

title: Judge's Power to Put Questions or Order Production
officialText: The Judge may ask any question, in any form, at any time, of any witness or party, and may order production of any document or thing in order to discover or obtain proper proof of relevant facts.
simpleExplanation: The Judge has broad powers to ask questions and seek documents necessary for finding the truth.
examples: A Judge directly questions a witness to clarify testimony.
A Court orders production of additional records.

faqs.question: Can a Judge directly question witnesses?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Judge may ask questions to discover relevant facts.

title: Power of Jury or Assessors to Put Questions
officialText: Where assessors are appointed in accordance with law, they may put questions to witnesses through or with the permission of the Judge.
simpleExplanation: Persons assisting the Court may ask questions through the Judge when permitted by law.
examples: An assessor seeks clarification on a witness's testimony.
Questions are routed through the presiding Judge.

faqs.question: Can assessors ask questions directly?
faqs.answer: Only in accordance with the law and under the Judge's supervision.

title: No New Trial for Improper Admission or Rejection of Evidence
officialText: The improper admission or rejection of evidence shall not by itself be a ground for a new trial or reversal of a decision if sufficient evidence exists to justify the decision and no substantial injustice has occurred.
simpleExplanation: A case will not automatically be retried merely because some evidence was wrongly admitted or excluded, unless it affected the outcome.
examples: A minor evidentiary error that does not affect the final judgment.
A decision supported by ample independent evidence.

faqs.question: Will every evidentiary mistake result in a retrial?
faqs.answer: No. Only errors causing substantial injustice are likely to affect the outcome.
